title: Terahertz spin-current transparency through rough interfaces
authors: Jiří Jechumtál, Jakub Zázvorka, Ondřej Novák, Martin Rejhon, Peter Kubaščík, Lukáš Nowak, Petr Němec, Eva Schmoranzerová, Martin Veis, Lukáš Nádvorník, Zdeněk Kašpar
abstract: Spin transport across interfaces is critical for spintronic devices, yet remains difficult to probe on ultrafast timescales. We use terahertz emission spectroscopy on Co|Pt heterostructures whose interface roughness is tuned through the thickness of an underlying Au buffer layer, while leaving other growth parameters unchanged. From the measured THz electric field, we extract the interface spin-current transparency ts after correcting for the changes in sample impedance and optical absorption of the stack. Surprisingly, we find that ts decreases by only approximately 30% as the interface root-mean-square roughness and the lateral grain size both increase by a factor of three, with no measurable change in the THz spectrum. These results demonstrate that interfacial spin transport is relatively robust against morphological variations on ultrafast timescales, establishing terahertz emission spectroscopy as a reliable probe of spin dynamics across imperfect interfaces.
summary: This study shows that spin-current transparency in Co|Pt heterostructures is surprisingly resistant to changes in interface roughness and grain size. By using THz spectroscopy, the researchers demonstrate that ultrafast spin transport is robust against morphological variations, which is a positive sign for the development of large-scale spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how interface roughness (both vertical and lateral) affects the spin-current transparency (ts) in Co|Pt heterostructures at ultrafast (THz) frequencies.
main_result: Spin-current transparency decreases by only about 30% even when interface roughness and grain size increase by a factor of three, demonstrating that interfacial spin transport is relatively robust against morphological variations on ultrafast timescales.
method: Terahertz emission spectroscopy using femtosecond laser excitation, combined with AFM for roughness characterization and numerical FDTD simulations to account for sample impedance and optical absorption.
model_or_system: Co|Pt heterostructures with a variable thickness Au buffer layer (Au(5)|Co(2)|Pt(5)|Au(d)|Si) used as a spintronic THz emitter.
key_observables: THz electric field strength, THz spectrum, interface spin-current transparency (ts), RMS roughness, and lateral grain size (EDR).
important_parameters: Ultrafast (sub-picosecond) timescales, spin diffusion length in Pt, and interface roughness scales (0.3-10 nm).
main_assumptions: The platinum crystalline quality and spin Hall angle remain constant across all samples, and the spin voltage/current scales directly with optical absorption and transparency.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of THz emission mechanism; Fig 2: THz waveforms, frequency spectra, and AFM topography maps; Fig 3: Plots of impedance, absorption, and roughness parameters vs. Au thickness; Fig 4: Extraction of spin transparency vs. roughness parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interface roughness, describes the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents the characterization of morphology via AFM, details the THz spectroscopy results, and concludes with a discussion on the robustness of spin transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: THz emission from FM|HM samples with variable buffer layer roughness. A fs laser pulse excites a ferromagnetic layer and injects a spin-current pulse js into a heavy metal layer. js is affected by the FM|HM interface spin transparency ts. Two extremal cases are shown: without an Au buffer layer (smooth interface) and with a thick Au buffer layer creating a very rough interface.

FIG. 2: (a) Raw THz signals measured by electro-optical sampling from a set of samples Au(5)|Co(2)|Pt(5)|Au(d). (b) Spectra of THz waveforms from (a). Spectral dips come from water vapor absorption. The thickness of the Au buffer layer for the given sample is represented by the line colour shown on the colour bar. (c) Representative topography profiles of the Pt(5)|Au(d) set of samples; note that the vertical axis is on the nanometer scale, while the horizontal axis is on the micrometer scale. (d) Selected Atomic Force Microscopy scans of the Pt(5)|Au(d) set of samples. The topography maps were measured in contact mode, revealing the building of a columnar-like structure with increasing...

FIG. 3: Sample properties as a function of the Au buffer layer thickness d. (a) Measured total sample impedance Z(d). (b) Calculated relative change of energy absorbed in the FM layer AFM(d) normalized to the value at d = 0. (c) Magnitude of THz signal STHz. (d) Interface roughness EDR (lateral roughness). (e) RMS (vertical roughness) of the AFM map. (f) Interface surface area as a function of Au thickness d.

FIG. 4: Spin transparency ts extracted from STHz as a function of surface roughness parameters (a) EDR (lateral roughness) and (b) RMS (vertical roughness). Both cases show a clear negative correlation, with ts decreasing by ∼30% across the studied range.
